en.extract:
Horsfield's bronze cuckoo (Chalcites basalis) is a small cuckoo in the family Cuculidae. Its size averages 22 g (0.78 oz) and is distinguished by its green and bronze iridescent colouring on its back and incomplete brown barring from neck to tail. Horsfield's bronze cuckoo can be distinguished from other bronze cuckoos by its white eyebrow and brown eye stripe. The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is common throughout Australia preferring the drier open woodlands away from forested areas. This species was formerly placed in the genus Chrysococcyx.


Taxonomy
The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is one of five Australian species in the genus Chalcites (formerly Chrysococcyx) a type of parasitic bird, that parasitizes fairy-wrens primarily to raise their young.


Diet and behaviour

The main diet of the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is insects and they are nomadic, travelling to different regions of Australia to breed and find food. Small insects are taken from leaves, branches, caught on the wing and in breeding season, Horsfield's bronze cuckoos feed each other in a courtship ritual.
The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is known as a brood parasite, this means that they lay their eggs in a host species nest. They mainly parasitize the fairy-wrens in the genus Malurus. It has been well documented that the superb fairy-wren (Malurus cyaneus) and the splendid fairy-wren (Malurus splendens) are the two main species to bear host to the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo, although they may also parasitise other small Passeriformes including thornbills, warblers and scrub-wrens that can be utilised as a secondary host in certain locations.
Although the behavioural attributes of a host species may play a role in parasitism, it is thought that the female selects its host through imprinting, remembering the species that it was raised by and ultimately using that species to raise its brood.


Breeding
The Horsfield's bronze cuckoos are known to form monogamous pairs in the breeding season and occupy the same breeding territories as their host species; however, partnerships are short-lived as a female will only occupy the breeding territory for a few weeks, as another female takes her place, she may form a pairing with the same male. Females that leave a breeding site after several weeks may move to another site and continue to breed with another male, forming another bond in a new breeding territory. Breeding territories of the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo generally do not overlap giving rise to the possibility that a pair will defend an area through the season.


Range
The species is native to Australia and South East Asia, found in Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Malaysia, and southern Thailand. In 2024, the first known specimen was recorded in New Zealand.


Parasitism
As a brood parasite, the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo does not build its own nest but will use a host species' nest to lay its eggs. The breeding season for the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo relies on their host and they will lay one to mimic that of the fairy wren or thornbill's egg, an elongated pinkish-white egg, that is speckled with red-brown spots. The breeding season for the superb fairy-wren is between September and February and a female may have three consecutive broods in this time, allowing the cuckoo multiple attempts to parasitize this species. The female cuckoo may choose a breeding site with a high density of hosts, which allows extra opportunity for her success in parasitizing a nest successfully. Studies have shown at one site a female did not parasitize a territory with less than 23 breeding pairs of their primary host (Malurus cyaneus).
The egg of a Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is small for its size, evolving over time to mimic those of their host in what can be described as an evolutionary arms race between parasite and host. Also, the smaller the host for the cuckoo, the likelihood of successfully raising multiple broods thus the energy and nutrients needed to produce more smaller eggs than few larger eggs can be utilised more efficiently. Egg laying is very fast for the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo; it is able to lay an egg in under 6 seconds typically in the morning shortly after the host has laid. The adult cuckoo removes one egg each time she lays, only laying one egg per nest and replacing one host egg with one of her own.
Younger, semi-experienced females were generally selected over new and novice breeding females due to their success and experience. The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo chose females that would choose similar breeding sites to previous years and were likely to raise several broods in one season. Generally, the superb fairy wren will not reject the cuckoo's egg. Fairy-wrens make oval dome nests that can be dark inside, meaning it is harder for the fairy-wren to distinguish between its own egg and the host's egg. Furthermore, the mimicry in eggs from the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo has evolved over time and the parasite eggs are hard to distinguish except for their slight elongation and glossier finish.
The cuckoo chick hatches within 12 days of incubation, 2 days before the host egg, ejecting other eggs in the nests within two days of hatching, leaving the cuckoo the sole chick. As newly hatched cuckoo chicks eject host eggs they do not get to learn the host's begging call, but can possess begging call polymorphism, where nestlings produce the calls of their primary host. As the nestling grows it will be fed by the host parent and possibly the group, growing more rapidly until fledged.


Coevolutionary arms race
Counter-adaptations have been documented for host species and cuckoos alike, as each adapts to the other in a coevolutionary "arms race". Studies show that coevolutions happen at all stages of the growth cycle, not just the early stages.
Fairy-wrens have adapted some host defences to reduce parasitism. The high cost of hosting a parasitic species, in energy and genetics, drives the host to improve its defences, which in turn drive the parasite to improve its offences.
Among the host's defensive adaptations:

Hosts like to nest in large colonies, to cooperate in spotting cuckoos and chasing them away.
Helpers in large colonies provision the female so she can spend more time guarding the nest.
Hosts learn and recognise their own eggs, and abandon any that are in the nest before they have started their own.
Among the cuckoo's adaptations:

Mimicry of host eggs.
Eggs that are cryptic and therefore unable to be seen in the dark nest.
Thickened egg shells.
Efficiency in laying the egg, being able to lay an egg secretly and quickly while the host is absent.


Media

'''Horsfield's bronze cuckoo''' ('''''Chalcites basalis''''') is a small cuckoo in the family [[Cuculidae]]. Its size averages {{cvt|22|g}}<ref name=Brooker1989a>{{cite journal|last1=Brooker|first1=M.G.|last2=Brooker |first2=L.C.|title=Cuckoo Hosts in Australia|journal=Aust Zool Rev|date=1989a|volume=2|pages=1–67}}</ref> and is distinguished by its green and bronze iridescent colouring on its back and incomplete brown barring from neck to tail. Horsfield's bronze cuckoo can be distinguished from other bronze cuckoos by its white eyebrow and brown eye stripe.<ref name=Morcombe>{{cite book|last1=Morcombe|first1=Michael|title=Field Guide to Australian Birds|date=2003|publisher=Pascal Press|location=Australia|isbn=978-1-74021-417-9|page=192|edition=second}}</ref> The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is common throughout Australia preferring the drier open woodlands away from forested areas.<ref name=Brooker1992>{{cite journal|last1=Brooker|first1=M.G|last2=Brooker|first2=L.C |title=Evidence for Individual Female Host Specificity in 2 Australian Bronze-Cuckoos (''Chrysococcyx'' Spp) |journal=Australian Journal of Zoology|date=1992|volume=40|issue=5|pages=485–493|doi=10.1071/zo9920485}}</ref> This species was formerly placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Chrysococcyx]]''.
  
==Taxonomy==
The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is one of five Australian species in the genus ''[[Chalcites]]'' (formerly ''[[Chrysococcyx]]'') a type of parasitic bird,<ref name=Morcombe /> that parasitizes fairy-wrens primarily to raise their young.<ref name=Brooker1989a /><ref name=langmore2007>{{cite journal|last1=Langmore|first1=N.E|last2=Kilner|first2=R.M|title=Breeding site and host selection by Horsfield's bronze-cuckoos ''Chalcites basalis'' |journal=Animal Behaviour|date=2007|volume=74|issue=4|pages=995–1004|doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2007.02.028|s2cid=53146815}}</ref>

==Diet and behaviour==
[[File:Horsfield's Bronze-Cuckoo Capertee.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Photographed at Capertee Valley, NSW, Australia]]
The main diet of the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is insects and they are nomadic, travelling to different regions of Australia to breed and find food.<ref name=langmore2007 /> Small insects are taken from leaves, branches, caught on the wing and in breeding season, Horsfield's bronze cuckoos feed each other in a courtship ritual.<ref name=Lorenzana1998>{{cite journal|last1=Lorenzana|first1=J.C|last2=Sealy|first2=S.G|title=Adult Brood Parasites Feeding Nestlings and Fledglings of Their Own Species: A Review|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|date=1998|pages=364–375}}</ref>

The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is known as a [[brood parasite]], this means that they lay their eggs in a [[host species]] nest.<ref name=Davies2010>{{cite book|last1=Davies|first1=N.B|title=Cuckoos, Cowbirds and Cheats|date=2010|publisher=A&C Black}}</ref> They mainly [[parasitize]] the fairy-wrens in the genus [[Malurus]]. It has been well documented that the [[superb fairy-wren]] (''Malurus cyaneus'') and the [[splendid fairy-wren]] (''Malurus splendens'') are the two main species to bear host to the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo, although they may also parasitise other small [[Passeriformes]] including [[Acanthiza|thornbills]], [[warblers]] and scrub-wrens that can be utilised as a secondary host in certain locations.<ref name=Brooker1989a /><ref name=langmore2007 /><ref name=Brooker1989b>{{cite journal|last1=Brooker|first1=M.G|last2=Brooker|first2=L.C|title=The comparative breeding behaviour of two sympatric cuckoos, Horsfield's Bronze‐Cuckoo ''Chrysococcyx basalis'' and the Shining Bronze‐Cuckoo ''C. lucidus'', in Western Australia: a new model for the evolution of egg morphology and host specificity in avian brood parasites|journal=Ibis|date=1989b|volume=131|issue=4|pages=528–547|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1989.tb04789.x}}</ref><ref name=Langmore2009a>{{cite journal|last1=Langmore|first1=N.E|last2=Kilner|first2=R.M|title=Why do Horsfield's bronze-cuckoo ''Chalcites basalis'' eggs mimic those of their hosts?|journal=Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology|date=2009a|volume=63|issue=8|pages=1127–1131|doi=10.1007/s00265-009-0759-9|s2cid=24883682}}</ref>
Although the behavioural attributes of a host species may play a role in [[parasitism]], it is thought that the female selects its host through imprinting, remembering the species that it was raised by and ultimately using that species to raise its [[Offspring|brood]].<ref name=Lack1963>{{cite journal|last1=Lack|first1=D|title=Cuckoo hosts in England|journal=Bird Study|date=1963|volume=10|issue=4|pages=185–203|doi=10.1080/00063656309476050|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Brooker1990>{{cite journal|last1=Brooker|first1=L.C|last2=Brooker|first2=M.G|title=Why are cuckoos host specific?|journal=Oikos|date=1990|pages=301–309|doi=10.2307/3565958|volume=57|issue=3|jstor=3565958}}</ref>

==Breeding==
The Horsfield's bronze cuckoos are known to form [[monogamy in animals|monogamous]] pairs in the breeding season and occupy the same breeding territories as their host species; however, partnerships are short-lived as a female will only occupy the breeding territory for a few weeks, as another female takes her place, she may form a pairing with the same male.<ref name=langmore2007 /> Females that leave a breeding site after several weeks may move to another site and continue to breed with another male, forming another bond in a new breeding territory. Breeding territories of the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo generally do not overlap giving rise to the possibility that a pair will defend an area through the season.<ref name=Langmore2009a />

==Range==

The species is native to Australia and South East Asia, found in Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Malaysia, and southern Thailand.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://ebird.org/species/hobcuc1?siteLanguage=en_NZ |title=Horsfield's Bronze-Cuckoo |website=eBird |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=25 August 2025}}</ref> In 2024, the first known specimen was recorded in New Zealand.<ref>{{cite Q|Q134485803}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aucklandmuseum.com/discover/collections/record/1349151 |title=Chrysococcyx basalis |website=[[Auckland War Memorial Museum]] |access-date=25 August 2025}}</ref>

==Parasitism==
As a brood parasite, the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo does not build its own nest but will use a host species' nest to lay its eggs. The breeding season for the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo relies on their host and they will lay one to [[mimic]] that of the fairy wren or thornbill's egg,<ref name=Brooker1989a /><ref name=Langmore2003>{{cite journal|last1=Langmore|first1=N.E|last2=Hunt|first2=S|last3=Kilner|first3=R.M|title=Escalation of a co-evolutionary arms race through host rejection of brood parasitic young.|journal=Nature|date=2003|volume=422|issue=6928|pages=157–160|doi=10.1038/nature01460|pmid=12634784|bibcode=2003Natur.422..157L|s2cid=4428447}}</ref> an elongated pinkish-white egg, that is speckled with red-brown spots. The breeding season for the [[superb fairy-wren]] is between September and February and a female may have three consecutive broods in this time, allowing the cuckoo multiple attempts to parasitize this species.<ref name=Cockburn2008>{{cite journal|last1=Cockburn|first1=A|last2=Sims|first2=R.A|last3=Osmond|first3=H.L|last4=Green|first4=D.J|last5=Double|first5=M.C|last6=Mulder|first6=R.A|title=Can we measure the benefits of help in cooperatively breeding birds: the case of Superb Fairy-wrens Malurus cyaneus.|journal=Journal of Animal Ecology|date=2008|volume=77|issue=3|pages=430–438|doi=10.1111/j.1365-2656.2007.01351.x|pmid=18312341|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Langmore2010>{{cite journal|last1=Langmore|first1=N.E|last2=Kilner|first2=R.M|title=The coevolutionary arms race between Horsfield's Bronze-Cuckoos and Superb Fairy-wrens |journal=Emu|date=2010|volume=110|issue=1|pages=32–38|doi=10.1071/mu09032|s2cid=86190974}}</ref> The female cuckoo may choose a breeding site with a high density of hosts, which allows extra opportunity for her success in parasitizing a nest successfully. Studies have shown at one site a female did not parasitize a territory with less than 23 breeding pairs of their primary host ''(Malurus cyaneus'').<ref name=langmore2007 />

The [[Bird egg|egg]] of a Horsfield's bronze cuckoo is small for its size, evolving over time to mimic those of their host in what can be described as an evolutionary [[arms race]] between parasite and host. Also, the smaller the host for the cuckoo, the likelihood of successfully raising multiple broods thus the [[energy]] and [[nutrients]] needed to produce more smaller eggs than few larger eggs can be utilised more efficiently.<ref name=Brooker1996>{{cite journal|last1=Brooker|first1=L|last2=Brooker|first2=M|title=Acceptance by the splendid fairy-wren of parasitism by Horsfield's bronze-cuckoo: further evidence for evolutionary equilibrium in brood parasitism |journal=Behavioral Ecology|date=1996|volume=7|issue=4|pages=395–407|doi=10.1093/beheco/7.4.395|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Payne1974>{{cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=R.B|title=The evolution of clutch size and reproductive rates in parasitic cuckoos|journal=Evolution|date=1974|volume=28|issue=2|pages=169–181|doi=10.2307/2407320|pmid=28563269|jstor=2407320|hdl=2027.42/137250|url=https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/2027.42/137250/1/evo00738.pdf|hdl-access=free}}</ref> Egg laying is very fast for the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo; it is able to lay an egg in under 6 seconds typically in the morning shortly after the host has laid.<ref name=Brooker1988>{{cite journal|last1=Brooker|first1=M.G|last2=Brooker|first2=L.C|last3=Rowley|first3=I|title=Egg deposition by the bronze-cuckoos ''Chrysococcyx basalis'' and ''Ch. lucidus''|journal=Emu|date=1988|volume=88|issue=2|pages=107–109|doi=10.1071/mu9880107}}</ref> The adult cuckoo removes one egg each time she lays, only laying one egg per nest and replacing one host egg with one of her own.<ref name=Langmore2010 />

Younger, semi-experienced females were generally selected over new and [[novice]] breeding females due to their success and experience. The Horsfield's bronze cuckoo chose females that would choose similar breeding sites to previous years and were likely to raise several broods in one season.<ref name=Brooker1996 /> Generally, the [[superb fairy wren]] will not reject the cuckoo's egg. Fairy-wrens make oval dome nests that can be dark inside, meaning it is harder for the fairy-wren to distinguish between its own egg and the host's egg. Furthermore, the mimicry in eggs from the Horsfield's bronze cuckoo has evolved over time and the parasite eggs are hard to distinguish except for their slight elongation and glossier finish.<ref name=Brooker1989a /><ref name=Langmore2009a /><ref name=Langmore2003 />

The cuckoo chick hatches within 12 days of [[Egg incubation|incubation]], 2 days before the host egg, ejecting other eggs in the nests within two days of hatching, leaving the cuckoo the sole chick. As newly hatched cuckoo chicks eject host eggs they do not get to learn the host's begging call, but can possess begging call polymorphism, where nestlings produce the calls of their primary host.<ref name=Brooker1989b /><ref name=Langmore2003 /><ref name=Langmore2008>{{cite journal|last1=Langmore|first1=N.E|last2=Maurer|first2=G|last3=Adcock|first3=G.J|last4=Kilner|first4=R.M|title=Socially Acquired Host-Specific Mimicry and the Evolution of Host Races in Horsfield's Bronze Cuckoo ''Chalcites basalis'' |journal=Evolution|date=2008|volume=62|issue=7|pages=1689–1699|doi=10.1111/j.1558-5646.2008.00405.x|pmid=18419751|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Payne1998>{{cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=R.B|last2=Payne|first2=L.L|title=Nestling eviction and vocal begging behaviors in the Australian glossy cuckoos ''Chrysococcyx basalis'' and ''C. lucidus'' |journal=Oxford Ornithology Series|date=1998|volume=9|pages=152–172}}</ref> As the nestling grows it will be fed by the host parent and possibly the group, growing more rapidly until [[fledged]].

==Coevolutionary arms race==
Counter-adaptations have been documented for host species and cuckoos alike, as each adapts to the other in a [[coevolution]]ary "arms race".<ref name=Dawkins1979>{{cite journal|last1=Dawkins|first1=R|last2=Krebs|first2=J.R|title=Arms races between and within species.|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences|date=1979|volume=205|issue=1161|pages=489–511|doi=10.1098/rspb.1979.0081|pmid=42057|bibcode=1979RSPSB.205..489D|s2cid=9695900}}</ref> Studies show that coevolutions happen at all stages of the growth cycle, not just the early stages.<ref name=Welbergen2009>{{cite journal|last1=Welbergen|first1=J.A|last2=Davies|first2=N.B|title=Strategic variation in mobbing as a front line of defense against brood parasitism|journal=Current Biology|date=2009|volume=19|issue=3|pages=235–240|doi=10.1016/j.cub.2008.12.041|pmid=19185495|s2cid=2619455|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Feeney2012>{{cite journal|last1=Feeney|first1=W.E|last2=Welbergen|first2=J.A|last3=Langmore|first3=N.E|title=The frontline of avian brood parasite–host coevolution|journal=Animal Behaviour|date=2012|volume=84|issue=1|pages=3–12|doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2012.04.011|s2cid=53201268}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Feeney |first1=William E.|last2=Welbergen |first2=Justin A. |last3=Langmore |first3=Naomi E. |date=2014 |title=Advances in the Study of Coevolution Between Avian Brood Parasites and Their Hosts |journal=Annual Review of Ecology, Evolution, and Systematics |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=227–246 |doi=10.1146/annurev-ecolsys-120213-091603|hdl=1885/66602 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

Fairy-wrens have adapted some host defences to reduce parasitism. The high cost of hosting a parasitic species, in energy and [[genetics]], drives the host to improve its defences, which in turn drive the parasite to improve its offences.<ref name=Kilner2011>{{cite journal|last1=Kilner|first1=R.M|last2=Langmore|first2=N.E|title=Cuckoos versus hosts in insects and birds: adaptations, counter‐adaptations and outcomes|journal=Biological Reviews|date=2011|volume=86|issue=4|pages=836–852|doi=10.1111/j.1469-185x.2010.00173.x|pmid=21223481|hdl=1885/64168|s2cid=3889366|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

Among the host's defensive adaptations:
*Hosts like to nest in large [[Colony (biology)|colonies]], to cooperate in spotting cuckoos and chasing them away.<ref name=Brown2007>{{cite journal|last1=Brown|first1=M|last2=Lawes|first2=M.J|title=Colony size and nest density predict the likelihood of parasitism in the colonial southern red bishop Euplectes orix–diderick cuckoo ''Chrysococcyx caprius'' system|journal=Ibis|date=2007|volume=149|issue=2|pages=321–327|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.2006.00633.x}}</ref><ref name=Welbergen2009/>
*Helpers in large colonies provision the female so she can spend more time guarding the nest.<ref name=Canestrari2009>{{cite journal|last1=Canestrari|first1=D|last2=Marcos|first2=J.M|last3=Baglione|first3=V|title=Cooperative breeding in carrion crows reduces the rate of brood parasitism by great spotted cuckoos|journal=Animal Behaviour|date=2009|volume=77|issue=5|pages=1337–1344|doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2009.02.009|s2cid=53171888}}</ref>
*Hosts learn and recognise their own eggs, and abandon any that are in the nest before they have started their own.<ref name=Langmore2003 /><ref name=Brooker1996 />

Among the cuckoo's adaptations:
*[[Mimicry]] of host eggs.<ref name=Feeney2012 /><ref name=Aviles2010>{{cite journal|last1=Aviles|first1=J.M|last2=Vikan|first2=J.R|last3=Fossoy|first3=F|last4=Antonov|first4=A|last5=Moksnes|first5=A|last6=Roskaft|first6=E|last7=Stokke|first7=B.G|title= Avian colour perception predicts behavioural responses to experimental brood parasitism in chaffinches|journal=Journal of Evolutionary Biology|date=2010|volume=23|issue=2|pages=293–301|doi=10.1111/j.1420-9101.2009.01898.x|pmid=20002251|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Spottiswoode2010>{{cite journal|last1=Spottiswoode|first1=C.N|last2=Stevens|first2=M|title=Visual modeling shows that avian host parents use multiple visual cues in rejecting parasitic eggs|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|date=2010|volume=107|issue=19|pages=8672–8676|doi=10.1073/pnas.0910486107|pmid=20421497|pmc=2889299|bibcode=2010PNAS..107.8672S|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Stoddard2010>{{cite journal|last1=Stoddard|first1=M.C|last2=Stevens|first2=M|title=Pattern mimicry of host eggs by the common cuckoo, as seen through a bird's eye|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|date=2010|doi=10.1098/rspb.2009.2018|volume=277|issue=1686|pages=1387–1393|pmid=20053650|pmc=2871939}}</ref>
*Eggs that are [[crypsis|cryptic]] and therefore unable to be seen in the dark nest.<ref name=Langmore2009b>{{cite journal|last1=Langmore|first1=N.E|last2=Stevens|first2=M|last3=Maurer|first3=G|last4=Kilner|first4=R.M|title= Are dark cuckoo eggs cryptic in host nests?|journal=Animal Behaviour|date=2009b|volume=78|issue=2|pages=461–468|doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2009.06.003|s2cid=54230252}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Marchant|first1=S|title=Evolution of the genus Chrysococcyx.|journal=Ibis|date=1972|volume=114|issue=2|pages=219–233|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1972.tb02604.x}}</ref>
*Thickened egg shells.<ref name=Brooker1991>{{cite journal|last1=Brooker|first1=MG|last2=Brooker|first2=L.C|title=Eggshell strength in cuckoos and cowbirds|journal=Ibis|date=1991|volume=133|issue=4|pages=406–413|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1991.tb04589.x}}</ref><ref name=Antonov2009>{{cite journal|last1=Antonov|first1=A|last2=Stokke|first2=B.G|last3=Moksnes|first3=A|last4=Roskaft|first4=E|title=Evidence for egg discrimination preceding failed rejection attempts in a small cuckoo host|journal=Biology Letters|date=2009|volume=5|issue=2|pages=169–171|doi=10.1098/rsbl.2008.0645|pmid=19126530|pmc=2665817}}</ref>
*Efficiency in laying the egg, being able to lay an egg secretly and quickly while the host is absent.<ref name=Langmore2010 /><ref name=davies1988>{{cite journal|last1=Davies|first1=N.B|last2=Brooke|first2=M.D.L|title=Cuckoos versus reed warblers: adaptations and counteradaptations|journal=Animal Behaviour|date=1988|volume=36|issue=1|pages=262–284|doi=10.1016/s0003-3472(88)80269-0|s2cid=53191651}}</ref>
